https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/558.failure_mode: Propellant migration and differential draw occurred between multiple hydrazine tanks on a series of geosynchronous satellites. Check valves with differing reseat pressures caused propellant to transfer from higher-reseat-pressure tanks to lower-reseat-pressure tanks following orbit-raising maneuvers, altering spacecraft inertial properties and reducing useable propellant life.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/558.system_config: Propulsion subsystem of geosynchronous satellites (and interplanetary spacecraft such as Mars 98 lander); four separate hydrazine propellant tanks, each with an individual check valve in the pressurization system.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/558.operating_conditions: On-orbit operations during and after orbit-raising maneuvers performed while spacecraft was spinning; differential heating or cooling of tanks also contributed to propellant migration during cruise phases.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/558.root_cause: The use of four separate tanks with individual check valves having differing reseat pressures caused propellant to transfer toward tanks with the lowest reseat pressures after each maneuver. Spinning during orbit raising further moved the spacecraft spin axis toward the fuller tanks, potentially amplifying transfer. Differential thermal conditions on the tanks added to the migration problem.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/558.corrective_action: Design an appropriate set of pyro valves and latch valves to control propellant migration and differential draw when a propellant is stored in multiple tanks. This valve architecture should prevent uncontrolled inter-tank transfer that shifts spacecraft inertial properties or reduces useable propellant.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/16901.failure_mode: Unenclosed propellant tanks on Earth-orbiting spacecraft are at risk of micrometeoroid and orbital debris (MMOD) strikes that can cause tank breach, loss of propellant, or catastrophic tank rupture. For the SMAP spacecraft, the propellant tank was only partially enclosed by the spacecraft structure, leaving its base exposed to space, and initial analysis showed the risk of impact-induced tank rupture was not in compliance with NASA requirements.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/16901.system_config: SMAP (Soil Moisture Active-Passive) spacecraft propulsion subsystem; spherical titanium propellant tank with elastomeric diaphragm expulsion device holding 81 kg of hydrazine, pressurized pre-launch and operated in mono-propellant blowdown mode; tank partially enclosed by spacecraft structure with expulsion outlet and dome oriented outward and an offset thermal blanket attached.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/16901.operating_conditions: Earth orbit (low Earth orbit) during the 3-year or longer mission, launched January 2015; orbit characterized by high orbital debris flux; tank exposed at base via open end of the launch vehicle interface during on-orbit operations.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/16901.root_cause: The original spacecraft design left the propellant tank only partially enclosed by the spacecraft structure, exposing the tank base to space. Multi-layer insulation (MLI) shielding, the typical MMOD mitigation, was rendered insufficient because the updated NASA Orbital Debris Engineering Model (ORDEM 3.0, 2014) introduced steel debris into the model, making MLI ineffective as a shielding wall and indicating a significantly higher risk of MMOD damage than previously estimated.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/16901.corrective_action: JPL added a hemispherical aluminum tank cap/cover to protect the exposed SMAP propellant tank. The recommendation is to consider placing metal or COPV propellant tanks destined for Earth orbit behind existing spacecraft structure or a dedicated tank cover, depending on orbit and other factors. Designers should be aware of the updated ORDEM 3.0 debris model. Additionally, safing of other stored energy sources (batteries, reaction wheels, etc.) should be assured for planned post-mission disposal operations. JPL incorporated this lesson into JPL standard D-17868 Rev. 6, Paragraph 4.1.5.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/205.failure_mode: A Hardware Interface Module (HIM) failed during pre-launch propellant loading of the STS 51-L mission, causing a two hour, 20 minute launch delay. The failure resulted in the complete loss of all fire detection and hazardous gas measurements required during propellant loading.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/205.system_config: External Tank (ET) instrumentation system, specifically the Hardware Interface Module (HIM), which interfaces ET sensors with launch control computer systems. The ET instrumentation comprises 38 sensors controlling ullage temperature and pressure, liquid level, LH2 depletion, and vent valve position.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/205.operating_conditions: Pre-launch propellant loading operations for the STS 51-L mission at KSC. The HIM failure occurred during ground operations while propellant was being loaded into the External Tank.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/205.root_cause: The HIM failed during propellant loading with no backup system available to maintain monitoring capability. The single-point failure of the HIM eliminated all fire detection and hazardous gas measurement visibility needed to detect propellant leaks and fire during loading operations.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/205.corrective_action: Provide a back-up system or module to: (a) preclude the inability to monitor measurements taken during loading and (b) prevent propellant loading delays caused by a single HIM failure.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/485.failure_mode: The Mars Observer mission failure was most probably caused by migration of substantial amounts of nitrogen tetroxide (NTO) oxidizer upstream through leaking check valves, where it condensed on cold pressurization tubing. When the pressurization sequence was executed upon Mars encounter, the liquid NTO may have mixed rapidly with monomethylhydrazine (MMH) fuel in the pressurization lines and detonated, potentially rupturing the tubing and causing loss of the spacecraft.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/485.system_config: Mars Observer bipropellant propulsion system; pressurization subsystem employing helium to maintain constant propellant tank pressure; pressure regulators and check valves separating the oxidizer tank (nitrogen tetroxide) and fuel (monomethylhydrazine) from the helium pressurization lines; cold pressurization plumbing inherited from an Earth-orbital design.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/485.operating_conditions: Interplanetary cruise phase of a planetary (Mars) mission; high propellant usage not required until months after launch; pressurization plumbing remained cold for much of the cruise; failure scenario triggered when the pressurization sequence was executed upon Mars encounter.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/485.root_cause: The pressurization system design was inherited from an Earth-orbital application where isolation from the oxidizer tank is achieved early in the mission after final orbit is reached. For a planetary mission, the long cruise phase with no high flow requirements allowed substantial NTO to migrate upstream through nominally leaking check valves. The cold pressurization plumbing caused the migrated NTO vapor to condense into liquid, creating conditions for a hypergolic reaction with MMH fuel when pressurization was reinitiated. Thermal analysis of the pressurization system to identify propellant condensation conditions had not been performed.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/485.corrective_action: Interplanetary missions should provide a means to positively isolate the pressurization system from the propellant tanks when high flow rates are not required, or ensure that propellant vapor migration into the pressurization system is benign. Thermal analysis of pressurization systems should be performed to identify incipient conditions for propellant condensation. JPL has incorporated these lessons into its design standard (JPL Document D-17868, Rev. 3) under paragraphs addressing passive isolation in bi-propellant systems and temperature control design for propellant vapor migration.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/206.failure_mode: The Inertial Upper Stage (IUS) Propellant Tank Assemblies (PTAs) lack adequate monitoring during flight, leaving IUS status uncertain. Because only one of two PTAs can be actively monitored during the countdown phase and the PTAs are isolated from each other until after IUS deployment, flight-induced anomalies such as propellant leaks cannot be detected.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/206.system_config: Inertial Upper Stage (IUS) reaction control system, specifically the Propellant Tank Assemblies (PTAs), as part of the IUS/Tracking and Data Relay Satellite (IUS/TDRS) configuration.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/206.operating_conditions: During the countdown phase (pre-launch) and flight operations; PTAs remain isolated from each other until activated after IUS deployment, limiting monitoring to only one of the two PTAs during countdown.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/206.root_cause: The IUS/TDRS instrumentation design allows active monitoring of only one of two PTAs during the countdown phase. The PTAs are isolated from each other until post-deployment activation, providing no indicator to detect flight-induced anomalies such as propellant leaks in unmonitored PTAs.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/206.corrective_action: Provide individual pressure and temperature monitoring of each IUS reaction control system PTA to enable detection of potential propellant leakage failures during all mission phases.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1334.failure_mode: Diaphragm propellant tanks on the Genesis spacecraft exhibited particulate contamination levels and non-volatile residue (NVR) that significantly exceeded the cleanliness specification after being filled with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for modal and acoustic vibration testing. The NVR contamination resulted from IPA leaching substances out of the AF-E-332 rubber diaphragm material following prolonged exposure, while excess particulate was attributed to imperfect cleaning and/or self-generation from the diaphragm rubbing against the tank wall.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1334.system_config: Genesis spacecraft propellant tanks; titanium sphere diaphragm-type propellant tank with an AF-E-332 rubber diaphragm; similar Atlas Centaur propulsion system hydrazine tanks. Propulsion system filters downstream of tanks also involved.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1334.operating_conditions: Ground test conditions: tanks were filled with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as a referee fluid in place of hydrazine propellant for modal and acoustic vibration testing. IPA remained in the tanks for approximately ten days before draining. A cleanliness demonstration test was also performed on Atlas Centaur tanks after similar vendor cleaning processes.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1334.root_cause: The vendor used only 1 liter of IPA for the final assembly-level cleaning, which was insufficient to fully wet and agitate internal surfaces, resulting in residual particulate contamination. Additional particulate may have been generated by diaphragm rubbing on the tank wall and release of particles from faying surfaces during transportation. Prolonged IPA exposure (significantly longer than 4 hours) caused the AF-E-332 rubber diaphragm to leach out-of-specification NVR contaminant.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1334.corrective_action: Conservatively sized propulsion system filters should be placed immediately downstream of diaphragm propellant tanks to accommodate particulate levels exceeding specification. Vendor cleaning procedures should be reviewed to ensure adequate quantities of cleaning fluid are used to fully wet and agitate internal surfaces. IPA exposure of the diaphragm should be limited to no more than 4 hours, followed immediately by vacuum drying. Isopropyl alcohol should not be introduced into diaphragm-type propellant tanks for extended periods during system testing unless the tanks are subsequently re-cleaned, verified clean, and vacuum dried.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1777.failure_mode: The Dawn spacecraft's Composite Over-wrapped Pressure Vessel (COPV) xenon propellant tanks experienced multiple failures during fabrication and testing. The flight spare tank (S/N 009) failed during its first pressurization to proof pressure, and the qualification tank (S/N 008) burst at 2453 psig, 6.5% below the required minimum burst pressure of 2625 psig. The development tank (S/N 002) also experienced liner distortion during annealing cycles and failed a leak check following six proof cycles.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1777.system_config: Dawn spacecraft electric propulsion system; Composite Over-wrapped Pressure Vessel (COPV) xenon propellant tank consisting of a titanium liner made from two welded domes over-wrapped with graphite fiber-based composite; tank skirt with mounting bosses and bushings; heaters integrated into the tank assembly.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1777.operating_conditions: Failures occurred during ground fabrication and acceptance/qualification testing. The flight spare (S/N 009) failed on its first pressurization to proof pressure. The qualification tank (S/N 008) underwent 12 cycles to 2188 psig, 16 cycles to 1750 psig, and 24 cycles to 1250 psig before failing a burst test at 2453 psig. Liner welding was performed without vacuum annealing, deviating from standard manufacturing process.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1777.root_cause: Three root causes were identified: (1) The weld process omitted vacuum annealing because the liner manufacturer could not control the annealing process without tank collapse, leaving higher residual stresses in the weld and heat-affected zone (HAZ). (2) The ductility of the weld HAZ was lower than expected due to hourglass distortion from radial sinking at the weld, with strain-to-failure measurements indicating the HAZ could not tolerate strains suggested by the material handbook. (3) A never-bonded region existed between layers of composite over the weld, where low-angle helical wraps were not adequately bonded to hoop wraps, compromising axial strain control of the liner in the weld region and resulting in larger-than-expected liner strain.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1777.corrective_action: Complete the full flight qualification process before flight tanks are accepted for flight. Require annealing or stress relief of welded liners prior to composite wrapping. Require direct verification of the mechanical integrity of the composite overwrap. Deviations from specified manufacturing processes should be identified as significant project risks. JPL incorporated this lesson as additional rationale supporting Design Principles paragraph 4.7.4.1 on Fiber-Reinforced Composite Over Wrapped Pressure Vessels (JPL Document D-17868, Rev. 3).

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/420.failure_mode: The pressure regulator in the Viking Orbiter-1 (VO-1) Propulsion Subsystem began leaking after it was brought back on-line 265 days after the near-Earth Trajectory Correction Maneuver (TCM), when an explosive (pyro) isolation valve was opened prior to the near-Mars TCM. The leakage was detected by a continual rise in fuel and oxidizer tank pressures, indicating the regulator had become non-leak-tight during the coast period.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/420.system_config: Viking Orbiter-1 (VO-1) Propulsion Subsystem, specifically the pressure regulator, pressurant isolation pyro valve, propellant tanks, and pressurant tanks comprising the propulsion pressurization subsystem.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/420.operating_conditions: The failure occurred during interplanetary cruise flight, approximately 265 days after the near-Earth TCM, when the pressurant isolation pyro valve was actuated open to bring the regulator back on-line prior to the near-Mars Trajectory Correction Maneuver (TCM).
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/420.root_cause: Telemetry analysis ruled out a scratch in the regulator poppet or seat and housing weld failure. Remaining probable causes are: (1) corrosion and/or residue formation in the regulator circuit due to propellant migration and/or pyro valve blowby during the extended coast period, and (2) particulate contamination. The micro-scale effects of long-term propellant exposure on regulator internals were not adequately understood at the time of design.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/420.corrective_action: Investigate the micro-scale effects of long-term propellant exposure to better critique regulator design. Design corrective measures for propulsion pressurization subsystems should include: (1) using separate regulators for the fuel and oxidizer sides; (2) incorporating a bellows in the pyro valve to eliminate blowby; and (3) adding an isolation valve between the regulator and the propellant tank to minimize propellant migration into the regulator. JPL has incorporated this lesson as support for propulsion system gas regulator design requirements in JPL Design Principles standard D-17868.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/364.failure_mode: The Galileo spacecraft experienced a dangerous increase in internal Retro Propulsion Module (RPM) tank pressure due to interactions between spacecraft power dissipation and propellant tank loading. Excess electrical power during cruise phase was dissipated by shunt heaters located near the RPM tanks, raising tank temperatures and pressure to unsafe levels. The risk was exacerbated by a late-cycle increase in RPM tank load from 84% to 94%.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/364.system_config: Galileo Spacecraft; Retro Propulsion Module (RPM) propellant tanks; shunt heater circuit used for excess electrical power dissipation; spacecraft fault detection and control software.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/364.operating_conditions: Cruise phase of flight operations, during periods of low spacecraft power demand when excess electrical power was routed to shunt heaters near the RPM tanks; the tank load had been increased to 94% (near-full ullage), making the system highly sensitive to temperature rises.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/364.root_cause: Design optimizations made late in the spacecraft development cycle — specifically increasing the RPM tank load from 84% to 94% — were not fully analyzed for interactions with other subsystems. The resulting low ullage combined with heat dissipation from shunt heaters caused significant RPM tank pressure increases. The tight coupling between power dissipation requirements and tank pressure limits was not identified until the changes were already implemented.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/364.corrective_action: A thorough cross-disciplinary analysis should be conducted before making changes to any flight project element late in the development cycle or after the test program is well advanced. This analysis must be conducted in concert with designers of all affected elements to identify subtle but significant interactions. Undiscovered interactions may reduce flexibility to recover from failures, negate opportunities to enhance science return, or require complicated and costly operational workarounds.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/3698.failure_mode: During STS-2 Forward Reaction Control System (FRCS) servicing, the MD162 quick disconnect failed, causing oxidizer to spill down the side of the FRCS module. No automatic safing was available, operator information was limited, and spill response actions were slow. Manual hypergolic propellant loading procedures for OMS and RCS tanks were lengthy, error-prone, and hazardous to operators.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/3698.system_config: Space Shuttle Orbital Maneuvering System (OMS) and Reaction Control System (RCS) hypergolic propellant tanks, helium and gaseous nitrogen (GN2) bottles, ground support equipment (GSE) including the Fluid Distribution System (FDS), quick disconnects (MD162), and the Launch Processing System (LPS).
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/3698.operating_conditions: Ground servicing operations for propellant loading of Space Shuttle OMS and RCS tanks at Kennedy Space Center. The failure occurred during prelaunch ground operations for STS-2 while manually servicing the Forward Reaction Control System with hypergolic oxidizer.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/3698.root_cause: Manual control of hazardous hypergolic servicing operations was susceptible to human error and lacked automatic safing capability. When the MD162 quick disconnect failed during STS-2 FRCS servicing, there was no automated monitoring or safing response, limited operator information, and slow spill response—all resulting from the absence of automated sequencing and monitoring in the GSE and LPS.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/3698.corrective_action: Automated sequencers were developed based on manual procedures and GSE/orbiter requirements documents, first deployed for STS-26R. Sequencers included automatic configuration verification, continuous monitoring of critical instrumentation against redline values, automatic securing and safing actions (via the Reactive Control Logic module), and predetermined hold points. Recommendations include evaluating potential events at each stage during software tool development, specifying appropriate automated responses and recovery actions, using more capable computer systems (e.g., PLCs) than the LPS, and maintaining highly trained operators with mandatory high-fidelity simulation certification, since automation does not eliminate all risks.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/484.failure_mode: The Mars Observer propulsion system, designed following earth-orbiting communications satellite practices, had inadequate flexibility in pressurization system isolation and marginal control of propellant vapor migration. This design limitation may have contributed to the loss of the Mars Observer mission, with two likely failure scenarios being propellant reaction leading to pressurization system line rupture and tank rupture due to regulator leakage.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/484.system_config: Mars Observer spacecraft propulsion subsystem, specifically the pressurization system that regulates propellant tank pressures during main engine maneuvers; bipropellant design adapted from earth-orbiting satellite heritage.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/484.operating_conditions: Planetary (interplanetary) mission flight conditions requiring high propellant flow rates years after launch, contrasted with the earth-orbital design basis where the pressurization system is used for only a few days following launch before isolation from propellant tanks.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/484.root_cause: The Mars Observer propulsion system adopted an earth-orbital pressurization system design that was inherently unsuitable for a planetary mission. Earth-orbital designs are essentially impervious to regulator leakage and propellant vapor diffusion because the pressurization system is isolated shortly after launch, whereas planetary missions require the system to function for years. Contractor experience with earth-orbiting spacecraft was assumed to be directly applicable to interplanetary spacecraft design, and differences in operating environments were not adequately considered early in the project.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/484.corrective_action: Consider contractor experience in bipropellant spacecraft propulsion system design during source selection. Assure thorough technical oversight of contractors with limited experience in interplanetary spacecraft design to manage risk on one-of-a-kind developments. Differences in operating environments between earth-orbiting and interplanetary missions must be identified and addressed very early in project design. JPL has incorporated this lesson into Flight Project Practices (JPL DocID 58032) and the Design Principles standard (JPL Document D-17868) as guidance on engineering inheritance and propulsion system design.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/487.failure_mode: Incompatible materials in propulsion system components posed major risks to multiple missions. Tungsten carbide/nickel regulator balls corroded substantially after 28-day NTO exposure, copper-bearing braze and wax lubricant in flow restrictor orifices were incompatible with NTO propellant vapor risking orifice plugging and pressure regulation failure, and Teflon valve bushings and seals swelled after prolonged oxidizer exposure causing significant check valve degradation. These failure modes are believed to have potentially contributed to the loss of Mars Observer.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/487.system_config: Propulsion pressurization systems on Cassini, Mars Global Surveyor (MGS), Mars Observer (MO), and Galileo spacecraft. Components involved include pressure regulators (tungsten carbide/nickel regulator balls, flow restrictors with AMS-4774 copper-bearing braze and wax lubricant), check valves (TFE Teflon bushings and PFA Teflon seals), and a 400 Newton main engine. Propellants are nitrogen tetroxide (NTO) oxidizer and monomethyl hydrazine (MMH) fuel with helium pressurant.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/487.operating_conditions: Long-duration interplanetary missions where propulsion pressurization components are exposed to propellant vapors for extended periods (weeks to months), unlike short Earth-orbital missions where pressurization is only required briefly after launch. Failures manifested during JPL qualification testing (28-day NTO exposure for Cassini regulator ball) and during flight operations (Galileo main engine delta-V maneuvers; MO in-flight loss).
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/487.root_cause: Materials used in heritage propulsion components were not tested or qualified for long-term chemical compatibility with propellants under interplanetary mission durations. Incompatible materials (tungsten carbide/nickel ball, copper-bearing braze, wax lubricant) were not listed on regulator materials lists and were never exposed to propellant vapors for sufficient duration on prior Earth-orbital missions to reveal degradation. Non-metallic components (Teflon) also swell upon prolonged oxidizer exposure even absent chemical incompatibility, causing functional degradation. Heritage systems were adopted for planetary missions without requalification for the new, more demanding environment.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/487.corrective_action: Propulsion system components should be tested for long-term chemical compatibility with propellants, performed by JPL or a qualified contractor if the component manufacturer lacks propellant experience. Heritage systems must be requalified to verify functionality in mission environments for which they were not originally intended. The incompatible materials (copper-bearing braze, wax lubricant) were eliminated in the MGS and Cassini regulator designs, and an alternative pressure regulator design was installed on Cassini. JPL incorporated this lesson as rationale in Flight Project Practices (JPL DocID 58032, Rev. 7) and Design Principles (JPL D-17868, Rev. 3) standards, specifically covering materials/processes/contamination control, inheritance practices, and propulsion system design guidelines.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1300.failure_mode: Six pressure transducers in the SIRTF propulsion subsystem failed during acceptance testing when a 500-volt insulation/isolation resistance test, intended only for propellant line heaters and tank heaters, was mistakenly applied to all electrical connections in the subsystem. The transducer circuitry had a maximum allowable test voltage of 50 volts, and application of 500 volts caused their damage.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1300.system_config: Space Infrared Telescope Facility (SIRTF) propulsion subsystem; components involved include propellant line pressure transducers, propellant line heaters, and tank heaters tested at a contractor facility during acceptance test.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1300.operating_conditions: Contractor facility acceptance test; electrical insulation/isolation resistance test conducted at 500 volts DC applied across all electrical connections in the propulsion subsystem.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1300.root_cause: An error in the acceptance test procedure (ATP) caused the 500-volt insulation/isolation resistance test—intended only for heaters—to be applied to the entire subsystem. A careful, detailed review of the ATP by the lead propulsion engineer was not performed, allowing the circuit voltage compatibility error to go undetected.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/1300.corrective_action: Test procedures should specify all voltage limitations to protect flight hardware. Test procedures must be approved by the cognizant subsystem engineer, confirming a careful, detailed review for both functionality and to preclude personnel injury or hardware damage. JPL incorporated this lesson as additional rationale in JPL standard 'Flight Project Practices, Rev. 7,' paragraph 6.12.5.3, regarding protection and security of flight hardware.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/642.failure_mode: A commercial communications satellite launched in 1989 experienced an unanticipated roll torque caused by residual magnetization of its stainless steel propellant tanks. The magnetized tanks' dipole moment interacted with Earth's magnetic field, producing a torque that forced excessive thruster corrections until propellant was prematurely exhausted, resulting in mission failure.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/642.system_config: Commercial communications satellite; attitude control system (thrusters); two large stainless steel propellant tanks (substituted for titanium tanks due to schedule deadlines).
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/642.operating_conditions: On-orbit flight operations beginning after launch in 1989; spacecraft operating in Earth's magnetic field environment; tanks magnetized either during or after fabrication on the ground.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/642.root_cause: The stainless steel propellant tanks, although generally assumed to be non-magnetic, became residually magnetized either through working the sheet metal into their hemispheric shape during fabrication or through exposure to an external magnetic field. Because stainless steel was assumed non-magnetic, no magnetic testing was performed. The resulting dipole moment (greater than 580 A-m2) interacted with Earth's magnetic field to produce the observed roll torque.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/642.corrective_action: Do not rely solely on the known magnetic properties of constituent materials when determining the magnetic properties of an end product; consider fabrication methods, shape, and exposure to external fields. Controls for residual magnetization should include: imposing a dipole moment limit (e.g., degauss to less than 5 A-m2 using a 40 Gauss alternating field), performing magnetic testing on end items, maintaining a magnetically clean ground environment, and monitoring spacecraft field exposure with magnetic sensors during handling and ground transportation.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/915.failure_mode: During a north-south stationkeeping maneuver in July 1998, thruster firing was aborted due to satellite attitude instability caused by uneven thruster firing. The instability resulted from two-phase flow of liquid hydrazine and Helium pressurant gas to thrusters in the odd half-system, indicating that hydrazine in the affected tank was nearly depleted despite fuel logs suggesting sufficient propellant remained.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/915.system_config: Geosynchronous satellite propulsion system; pressurized hydrazine fuel tanks (odd and even half-systems); Helium pressurant gas; stationkeeping thrusters.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/915.operating_conditions: On-orbit operations during a north-south stationkeeping maneuver in July 1998; pressurized tank system with hydrazine fuel in near-depletion state.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/915.root_cause: The fuel log was maintained using a bookkeeping method in which mission analysis software calculated fuel used by each thruster based on propulsion and maneuver parameters, supplemented by telemetry-based calculations using tank temperature and pressure relative to initial conditions. Both methods provided overestimates of remaining fuel because uncertainty in remaining hydrazine mass increases with fuel depletion, leading operators to believe sufficient fuel remained for additional maneuvers when it did not.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/915.corrective_action: Satellite builders and operators should plan for thermal capacitance testing as an additional method of fuel gauging. Because propellant tank system thermal response is defined by mass and heat capacity, the rate of temperature rise due to a known heat input indicates remaining fuel quantity. Careful location of heaters and temperature sensors should allow prelaunch calibration and on-orbit thermal tests that provide propellant gauging with uncertainty that decreases rather than increases with depletion.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/77.failure_mode: During pressurization of the oxidizer propellant tank, an 8 psi change in ullage pressure caused the computer program to incorrectly indicate that the interface and supply valves were closed. An engineer, believing it was safe, inadvertently opened the dome load valve (instead of closing it), then opened the interface and supply valves, causing pressure to spike to approximately 400 psig and rupturing the burst disk.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/77.system_config: OMS/RCS (Orbital Maneuvering System/Reaction Control System) propellant pressurization system, including the oxidizer propellant tank, dome load valve, interface valve, supply valve, burst disk, and the computer program control logic monitoring the system.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/77.operating_conditions: Ground processing operations at KSC; the failure occurred during pressurization of the oxidizer propellant tank while the system was being monitored by computer control logic. The pressure online with the module was 190 psig and spiked to approximately 400 psig.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/77.root_cause: The OMS/RCS system program control logic limits were not adjusted or reset prior to starting the pressurization operation. Because the control logic does not reset each time the program is activated, it indicated an incorrect valve status, leading the engineer to believe valves were closed when they were not, resulting in inadvertent over-pressurization.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/77.corrective_action: Add a procedural step to all OMS/RCS system OMIs (Operations and Maintenance Instructions) that are monitored by control logic, requiring that logic limits be adjusted/reset prior to any change in pressure, to prevent inadvertent over-pressurization of the system.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/108.failure_mode: The helium injection system lost its ability to introduce controlled helium flow into the aft end of the feedline during propellant loading. Failure of the series valve (A78410) or clogging of the downstream filter could cause loss of helium bubbling, potentially resulting in geysering damage to the external tank (ET) prior to 2% full or after 98% full.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/108.system_config: Helium injection system supplied from panel S72-0697-08, consisting of one series valve (A78410) and two parallel valves, with a downstream filter, connected to the LOX feedline aft end.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/108.operating_conditions: During slow LOX fill propellant loading operations and holds before launch, specifically at external tank fill levels below 2% or above 98%.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/108.root_cause: Failure of the series helium injection valve (A78410) or clogging of the downstream filter in the helium injection system, causing loss of helium bubbling in the feedline.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/108.corrective_action: Develop a preventative maintenance program for the critical valves and filters in the helium injection system to prevent valve failure or filter clogging.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/6149.failure_mode: Residual hydrogen vapor was not fully removed from the hydrogen fill and drain line during the terminal countdown sequence due to helium purge injection points located several feet from the dead-headed end of the line. This incomplete purging created a potentially catastrophic condition and constituted a constraint to launch in the Space Shuttle Program.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/6149.system_config: Space Shuttle Program liquid hydrogen feed system; specifically the External Tank (ET) Ground Umbilical Carrier Plate (GUCP), the hydrogen high point bleed location, and the hydrogen fill and drain line. The subsystem involves hydrogen propellant lines purged with helium gas.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/6149.operating_conditions: Terminal countdown sequence during pre-launch operations; ground testing at Marshall Space Flight Center (MSFC) confirmed incomplete hydrogen vapor purging under these conditions.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/6149.root_cause: Helium purge lines were connected to hydrogen lines several feet upstream of the dead-headed end, leaving a length of piping between the injection point and the closed end that helium gas could not fully sweep. This plumbing design practice prevented complete removal of hydrogen vapor from the lines prior to launch.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/6149.corrective_action: For future designs requiring purging of hydrogen systems, purge lines should be connected as physically close as possible to the dead-headed end of the line to completely sweep unwanted vapor from the system. Design engineering should adopt this as a rule of thumb and best practice.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/4058.failure_mode: Space Shuttle Alkaline Fuel Cell Powerplants (FCPs) require higher purity LH2 (99.99%) and LO2 (99.989%) than Space Shuttle Main Engines, necessitating separate cryogenic loading operations and dedicated Power Reactant Storage and Distribution (PRSD) systems. The incompatibility between fuel cell grade and propellant grade reactants prevents a common cryogenic system from serving both propulsion and electrical power generation.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/4058.system_config: Space Shuttle Alkaline Fuel Cell Powerplants (FCPs) and Power Reactant Storage and Distribution (PRSD) system; Space Shuttle Main Engines; Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) FCPs; Environmental Control and Life Support System (ECLSS); Constellation Program Altair Lunar Surface Access Module FCPs.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/4058.operating_conditions: Design phase discussions for the Constellation Program Altair Lunar Surface Access Module and KSC FCP/PRSD engineer discussions with engineers from other centers during fuel cell research projects. The issue pertains to cryogenic loading operations during ground processing and surface lander mission operations.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/4058.root_cause: Space Shuttle Alkaline FCPs require fuel cell grade reactants at higher purity levels than propellant grade reactants used by main engines, requiring separate cryogenic systems and load operations. Technical issues include how to effectively eliminate residual pressurants (such as Helium) from propellant tanks and how to design FCPs to accommodate and remove additional impurities that would collect in the fuel cell if propellant grade reactants were used.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/4058.corrective_action: During the initial design phase for spacecraft utilizing FCPs, determine if FCPs can meet design requirements using propellant grade reactants. If feasible, perform a trade study to evaluate whether the advantages (reduced mass, cost, simplified cryogenic loading operations, consolidated cryo load) warrant such a design. Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) FCPs capable of using propellant grade reactants should be considered as alternatives to alkaline FCPs.

https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/168.failure_mode: A three-inch diameter stainless steel manifold containing hydrogen (0.27 lbs) and air (0.26 lbs) at 350 psia ruptured due to a detonation of an unplanned hydrogen and air mixture inside the manifold. Damage was restricted to the manifold and some of the experimental package valves.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/168.system_config: Propellant piping system consisting of a three-inch diameter stainless steel manifold with hydrogen and air supplies, experimental package valves, located within an altitude tank test apparatus.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/168.operating_conditions: Experimental operation during testing at simulated altitude conditions inside an altitude tank, with hydrogen and air at 350 psia; the test crew deviated from the preplanned test procedure while attempting to make the device work.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/168.root_cause: The principal cause was the lack of backflow prevention devices in the hydrogen and air supplies to the test package, which permitted the accumulation of a detonatable hydrogen-air mixture within the manifold. A contributing factor was deviation from the preplanned test procedure by the test crew.
https://llis.nasa.gov/lesson/168.corrective_action: Design backflow prevention devices into propellant piping systems to prevent the accumulation of combustible mixtures in confined areas. These recommendations were approved by the LERC executive safety board.
